| 緯度度 = 
| 経度度 = 
}}

'''Kamō no Kus''' (蒲生のクス, かもうのクス) is a giant {{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|qid=Q158722|lt=kusunoki|1=Camphora officinarum}} tree located within the precincts of {{ill|Kamō Hachiman Shrine|ja|蒲生八幡神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamō Hachiman Shrine|qq=}} in Kamō Town, upper Hisayutaka, in {{ill|Aira, Kagoshima|en|Aira, Kagoshima|qid=Q407947|lt=Aira City|1=Aira, Kagoshima}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|qid=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture|1=Kagoshima Prefecture}}. It has been designated as a National {{ill|Special Natural Monument|ja|特別天然記念物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Special Natural Monument|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天然記念物|comment=changed title based on wikidata|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天然記念物|comment=enwiki is a redirect|1=Natural monument|qid=Q23790}}.

== Overview ==
{{Location map+|Japan Kagoshima Prefecture|relief=1|width=240|float=right|caption=Location of {{ill|Kamō no Kus|ja|蒲生のクス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamō no Kus|1=Kamō no Kusu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11621332}}|places=
{{Location map~|Japan Kagoshima Prefecture|lat=31.765764|long=130.569442|position=left|label='''Kamō no Kus'''|mark=Red pog.svg}}
{{Location map~|Japan Kagoshima Prefecture|lat=31.596806|long=130.557139|position=left|label=<small>{{ill|Kagoshima|en|Kagoshima|qid=Q15674|lt=Kagoshima City|1=Kagoshima}}</small>|mark=Green pog.svg}}
}}
The tree stands approximately 30 meters tall, with a trunk circumference of 24.2 meters measured at a height of 1.3 meters above ground level, and a root spread of 33.5 meters. An internal cavity measuring about 13 square meters (equivalent to eight tatami mats) has been detected within the trunk. Its age is estimated at around 1500 years (as of {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|qid=Q234510|lt=Heisei|1=Heisei era}} 23 ({{ill|2011|en|2011|qid=Q1994|lt=2011|1=2011}})). A survey conducted by the {{ill|Environmental Agency|ja|環境庁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Environmental Agency|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 環境省|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 環境省|1=Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1125558}} in Showa 63 ({{ill|1988|en|1988|qid=Q2426|lt=1988|1=1988}}) recognized it as the largest giant tree in Japan. It is also sometimes referred to as "Kamō no Ōkus" (蒲生の大楠) or "Kamō no Ōkus" (蒲生の大クス).

== History ==
According to tradition, during the {{ill|Usa Hachiman Shrine Oracle Incident|ja|宇佐八幡宮神託事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usa Hachiman Shrine Oracle Incident|1=Dōkyō Incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11449036}}, {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|Wake no Kiyomaro|qid=Q7961005|lt=Waki Kiyomaro|1=Wake no Kiyomaro}}—exiled to Ōsumi—visited the area, and it is said that the staff (杖) he carried was driven into the ground and took root (a legend known as the {{ill|Tsue Tate Densetsu|ja|杖立て伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsue Tate Densetsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). It is further recorded that by the 4th year of the {{ill|Hōan|en|Hōan|qid=Q1194464|lt=Hōan era|1=Hōan}} (1123), when {{ill|Kamō Shunsei|ja|蒲生舜清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamō Shunsei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} founded Kamō Hachiman Shrine, the tree was already venerated as a sacred tree ({{ill|Shinboku|en|Shinboku|qid=Q48743901|lt=Shinboku|1=Shinboku}}), suggesting that it was already of considerable size at that time.<ref name="蒲生八幡神社">[http://www.kamou80000.com/okusu.html {{ill|Kamō Hachiman Shrine|ja|蒲生八幡神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamō Hachiman Shrine|qq=}} “Japan’s Largest Giant Tree”] (accessed October 16, 2021)</ref> In the 3rd year of the {{ill|Eishō (Muromachi period)|en|Eishō (Muromachi period)|qid=Q1193577|lt=Eishō Era|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} (1506), a fire broke out on January 7 in the forest of Hachiman, resulting in the burning of some branches.

In 1913 ({{ill|1913|en|1913|qid=Q2080|lt=Taisho 1|1=1913}}), the tree was featured in the "Great Japanese Old Trees Ranking" as the Eastern Yokozuna, and on March 8 of Taishō 11 it was designated a {{ill|Natural monument|en|Natural monument|qid=Q23790|lt=Natural Monument|1=Natural monument}}, and later, on March 29, 1952 ({{ill|1952|en|1952|qid=Q5272|lt=Showa 27|1=1952}}), it was designated as a {{ill|Special Natural Monument|ja|特別天然記念物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Special Natural Monument|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天然記念物|comment=changed title based on wikidata|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天然記念物|comment=enwiki is a redirect|1=Natural monument|qid=Q23790}}. On August 31, 1985 (Showa 60), due to strong winds from {{ill|Typhoon No. 13 of Showa 60|ja|昭和60th year 台風第13号|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Typhoon No. 13 of Showa 60|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, many branches and leaves were lost; however, through the protective efforts of local residents, the tree has recovered.

It is noted that another tree, the "Great Kus" of Izumi in {{ill|Izumi, Kagoshima|en|Izumi, Kagoshima|qid=Q15734|lt=Izumi City|1=Izumi, Kagoshima}}, which is designated as a municipal natural monument, is considered a kindred tree to Kamō no Kus, and a tragic love legend is associated with the two.<ref name="蒲生八幡神社"/>
